thinking about cursors strikes again.... In the case phil brought up where we have a sensor that is working through a backlog of 1000 asset materializations and launching jobs for 25 materializations per evaluation, we only want to update the cursor to those 25 materializations. But right now if you return any RunRequest the cursor gets updated to the latest materialization. So I think the `advance_cursor` functions need to be included in this PR, and we'll need to make it so that every sensor must manually handle the cursor updating logic.

alternately we could have a bool attribute on the context like `cursor_has_been_updated` that gets flipped to false at the beginning of the evaluation fn. any call to `advance_cursor` flips `cursor_has_been_updated` to true. Then after we have run the user's decorated function, we can check if a run request has been returned AND `cursor_has_been_updated` is false, and if so we can advance the cursor all the way to the latest materializations

Cherry-picked https://github.com/kubernetes-client/python/pull/1322 (just merged into master today) to rename the 1.17 snapshot. 

There should be no "snapshot" section in the CHANGELOG.md in a release branch. A "snapshot" section is a placeholder for a WIP release, it can only exists in the master branch, and should be renamed when we cut a release. 

The correct order should have been:
- master branch has 17.0.0-snapshot
- release-17.0 branch renames 17.0.0-snapshot into v17.14.0a1
- https://github.com/kubernetes-client/python/pull/1322 cherrypicks the rename back to master branch
- master branch has 18.0.0-snapshot (**this didn't happen, we did everything in release-18.0 and left the master branch behind**)
- release-18.0 branch renames 18.0.0-snapshot into v18.17.0a1 (**we are here**)
- cherrypick the rename back to master branch (**this is not possible yet. Master branch is still at 17.0.0-snapshot. I need to make the master branch catch up**)

I'm just pointing out the current env var set / read is hacky. It works, but it smells a bit patchy.

I posit that, for non-pantsd runs, no env var should be needed, the cwd is correct, say three levels deep in the repo. It's only for the same run under pantsd where cwd is incorrect. In that case pantsd is purposely demonized at the build root. But! In that exact and only case (the most common case to be sure), the nailgun protocol Pants uses to send the client request to the pantsd server already carries the client cwd as part of the protocol. We just neglected to use that. That's what I'm pointing out and suggesting you use, since it's purpose built for exactly this already. You may or may not still need an env var hack, but it should only be needed (set) exactly at the point the nail is being invoked and it should be set using the value already passed in the nailgun protocol (that the rust side of the nail neglects to pass to the Python side).

If the above paragraph is news, that's my point. You may still not want to use the existing mechanism, and that's fine I guess, but in that case the code setting the env var deserves a comment calling the shot - "We have the ng protocol for this but don't use it because...".

Well, at the time `AssetSelection` was designed, there was no user code associated with a source asset, no it didn't make sense for `AssetSelection` to capture them-- that's why none of the older `AssetSelection` methods select source assets.

But in a (future) world where source asset observations can run together with materializing assets, then it makes sense to be able to select them together. IMO the only reason we wouldn't do so for `groups` immediately when this functionality is available is that it would change existing jobs defined with this method-- so, yes, purely backcompat.

I think we might have issues here with large images and batches.

Imagine that the image is of size 1000x1000, and the boxes thus can be from 0 to 1000 in value. The quantized boxes (which have the same quantizers for all boxes) would not be able to properly index the 0, 1, 2, etc values needed for the image index in the batch.

I think the solution for this is either
- [easy] disable batch support altogether in the quantized version of the op
- [medium] try using per-channel quantization of the boxes (so that the quantizer for the indices is different than the quantizer for the coordinates) (need to see how it integrates with the rest of the pipeline)
- [harder] break BC in the kernels and pass instead a list of tensors for the rois. This would maybe involve more changes down the road in the Faster R-CNN model and would probably be tricky, so I wouldn't recommend this.

Additionally, can you also add a quick test exercising this code-path that I mentioned (with a large image and a few batch elements)?

@Morendil , merci pour ton retour. Avant 2018, les plus-values imposées à l'impôt sur le revenu étaient soit inclus au barème, soit soumises à des taux forfaitaires. La variable `taxation_plus_values_hors_bareme` calcule ces taxations forfaitaires pour les plus-values. Ces plus-values taxées forfaitairement peuvent entrer dans des bases ressources. La variable `plus_values_taxees_hors_bareme` calcule ces plus-values. Le problème, c'est que ces différentes plus-values sont taxées à des taux différents, d'où le fait qu'on ne peut pas déduire simplement `plus_values_taxees_hors_bareme` de `taxation_plus_values_hors_bareme`, ou vice-versa. La création de `plus_values_taxees_hors_bareme` va en fait simplifier beaucoup de choses à terme je pense pour l'inclusion des plus-values dans les différentes bases ressources. Cette PR le fait pour l'AAH. A terme, il faudrait le faire pour d'autres presta. Bref, en apparence, ce code est sous-optimal, mais je ne sais pas s'il y a une meilleure alternative, à part multiplier les variables de plus-values (une variable par type de plus-values taxée forfaitairement, sachant que ça change très souvent entre les années : ceci serait très complexe à lire). making the signaling more in-band by requiring the decorator to be
invoked as `@log_latency()` rather than `@log_latency`, but this has the

I think that rather than using the marker type for this,`HydrateSourcesRequest` should maybe have an API like the following:
```
class HydrateSourcesRequest:
    input: Sources
    output: Type[Sources] = Sources
```
ie: `HydrateSourcesRequest` would always have an output type to hydrate as, and the default output type would be `Sources`.

The output type would act as an assertion that the field was either: 1) already that type, 2) convertible to that type via codegen. The reason I think that this is important is that callers shouldn't need to be aware of the existence of codegen: so to the extent that it's possible, codegen should be invisible in the API other than in the rule that implements source hydration.

It is when `return` returns nothing, it ends the generator. Right now the generator is only early return because the controlling code in `nodes.rs` stops its loop when it receives its 1st non Get*. It could continue to advance the generator in this code and the python generator machinery would not stop early but continue on to the Gets.

For example:
```python
>>> def coroutine(exit_early):
...   if exit_early:
...     yield 42
...   yield 37
...   x = yield
...   y = yield
...   yield x * y
...   yield 1/137
... 
>>> cr_early = coroutine(True)
>>> next(cr_early)
42
>>> next(cr_early)
37
>>> next(cr_early)
>>> cr_early.send(3)
>>> cr_early.send(5)
15
>>> next(cr_early)
0
>>> next(cr_early)
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
StopIteration
```

Versus an early return forced by the controlled code:
```python
>>> def coroutine(exit_early):
...   if exit_early:
...     yield 42
...     return
...   yield 37
...   x = yield
...   y = yield
...   yield x * y
...   yield 1/137
... 
>>> cr_early = coroutine(True)
>>> next(cr_early)
42
>>> next(cr_early)
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
StopIteration
```

Versus the syntax error you're thinking of:
```python
>>> def coroutine(exit_early):
...   if exit_early:
...     yield 42
...     return 3
...   yield 37
...   x = yield
...   y = yield
...   yield x * y
...   yield 1/137
... 
  File "<stdin>", line 4
SyntaxError: 'return' with argument inside generator
```

```python
#!/usr/bin/env python
# Extremely Principled TLS v1.2 ClientHello parser for ALPN extensions

import struct
import binascii


_int16 = struct.Struct(">H")


def int16(b):
    """
    Return first two bytes of b as an unsigned integer.
    """
    return _int16.unpack(b[:2])[0]


def take(data, count):
    prefix = data[:count]
    data = data[count:]
    return prefix, data


# Handshake, 3, 1 (TLS 1.0)
HEADER = b"\x16\x03\x01"
TYPE_ALPN = b"\x00\x10"


def parseHello(data):
    """
    Parse TLS 1.2 ClientHello from data, return the extensions as binary data
    or None if not found.

    Likely to raise struct.error or IndexError on error.
    """
    # if not data.startswith(HEADER):
    #     return
    header, data = take(data, 7)
    messageId, major, minor, l1, l2 = struct.unpack(">BBBHH", header)
    print(messageId, major, minor, l1, l2)
    # if not data.startswith(b"\x01"):  # ClientHello
    #     return
    header2, data = take(data, 4)
    print(struct.unpack(">HBB", header2))  # inner length; 3; 3
    # random
    random, data = take(data, 32)
    # session identifier
    slen, data = take(data, 1)
    slen = slen[0]
    session, data = take(data, slen)
    # ciphers list
    clen, data = take(data, 2)
    clen = int16(clen)
    ciphers, data = take(data, clen)
    # compression methods (should always be an array of length 1, with one 0 element)
    compression_length, data = take(data, 1)
    compression_methods, data = take(data, compression_length[0])
    # extensions
    extlen, data = take(data, 2)
    extensions, data = take(data, int16(extlen))
    return extensions


def parseExtensions(data):
    """
    Yield (type, body) for TLS extensions in data, as binary data.
    """
    while data:
        type, data = take(data, 2)
        length, data = take(data, 2)
        body, data = take(data, int16(length))
        yield (type, body)


def parseAlpn(body):
    """
    Parse array of Pascal strings, ignore a 16-bit length header.
    """
    length, body = take(body, 2)
    while body:
        protocol, body = take(body[1:], body[0])
        yield protocol


if __name__ == "__main__":
    sample_hello = b""

    import base64
    import pprint

    data = bytearray(base64.b64decode(sample_hello))
    extensions = parseHello(data)
    for (type, body) in parseExtensions(extensions):
        if type == TYPE_ALPN:
            pprint.pprint(list(parseAlpn(body)))

```

> Right now, I think that you think that there are only two transaction types -- one for defining a smart contract (which has the side-effect of also instantiating a smart contract account), and one for calling a method in an existing smart contract (and does not instantiate a new smart contract account). Are you saying that we need to expand the second transaction type to be able to execute any Clarity code?

If this is an important use case, then yes, I believe we should.

> I'm strongly of the opinion that our transaction wire format should offer first-class support for post-conditions on the transaction's originating account outside of the Clarity code. In my SIP 005 draft, the rationale is:

I see. I think the disagreement here stems from viewing this as a post-condition. In my mind, the goal is to mimic for first-class assets the guarantees that any blockchain provides for its native token: maximum transfer guarantees at the wire format level -- not a general purpose ability to provide post-conditions (which is where things can get tricky, because we'll be fighting the urge to define a new domain-specific language for encoding post-conditions, and trying to draw lines on what kinds of data may be inputs to the post-conditions). If all we're doing is providing maximum transfer guarantees, then a lot of those design choices become much simpler.

> 100% agreed that there will be cases where we will want this. However, I think that post-conditions on a transaction's originating account (which is usually also the spending account) will be so common and offer such a huge security win that it's worth it to make them as cheap as possible to evaluate and as simple as possible to encode

Right -- this is an example of why we might want to provide a just a whitelist. It's relatively easy to encode different principals in a whitelist. If we wanted general post-conditions, supporting conditions on multiple principals would start to look very much like a new programming language.

The reason why we need to keep track of the number of stealable tasks as well as the number of tasks in flight (the former are a subset of the latter) is that the two quantities are often not equal, and using only one variable instead of two would cause some complications, inefficiencies, and perhaps errors.

When we steal a bunch of tasks from a worker, assuming that the reply to the thief's `StealWork` RPC arrives to the owner before the reply to the victim's `PushNormalTask` RPCs for the stolen tasks, we remove the stolen tasks from the `stealable_tasks_` hash_set, while the victim's `tasks_in_flight_` value initially remains unchanged. This reflects the fact that the stolen tasks are still technically in flight to the victim. Knowing how many tasks are in flight to a worker (even if they have been stolen) is important, for example, to know when to return the worker to the Raylet. So even if the stolen tasks are not going to be executed by the victim anymore, it is still useful for us to know that they are still "in flight", in the sense that their respective RPCs have not completed yet.

On the other hand, knowing the number of tasks in flight does not allow us, on its own, to perform work stealing in an efficient way. If we rely on it to choose which worker to steal from (picking the one with more tasks in flight), we will frequently make a suboptimal choice, as many times the number of tasks up for grab will be lower than the reported number of tasks in flight. 

Having decided to simultaneously keep track of the number of stealable tasks, as well as the number of tasks in flight to a worker, why use a `flat_hash_set` containing the TaskIDs of the `stealable_tasks_`, instead of just using an integer? The reason for this is to avoid a race condition between a thief's `StealWork` RPC callback and the victim's `PushNormalTask` RPC callbacks. When the thief steals some tasks from a victim, the reply to the `StealWork` RPC usually comes back to the owner earlier than the replies to the PushNormalTask RPCs. However, this might not always be the case. If the PushNormalTask callbacks are run first, they will be the ones responsible for decrementing the number of stealable tasks (to maintain the invariant that `stealable_tasks_.size() <= tasks_in_flight`). The challenge is that we must decrement the number of stealable tasks exactly once for each stolen task, and decrementing an integer is not an idempotent operation. On the other hand, deleting a `TaskID` from a set is idempotent, so we don't have to worry about finding a way to let the `PushNormalTask` and the `StealWork` callbacks coordinate to decrement the counter only once.

**delivery annotation spec**: http://docs.oasis-open.org/amqp/core/v1.0/os/amqp-core-messaging-v1.0-os.html#type-delivery-annotations

> The delivery-annotations section is used for delivery-specific non-standard properties at the head of the message. **Delivery annotations convey information from the sending peer to the receiving peer**. If the recipient does not understand the annotation it cannot be acted upon and its effects (such as any implied propagation) cannot be acted upon. Annotations might be specific to one implementation, or common to multiple implementations. > `first_channel_name` is confusing here since the function only needs the instrument name.

`first_channel_name` is not only used to extract the instrument name prefix, it is also used to determine whether the session is an independent-channels session (only independent-channels session would return `first_channel_name` with instrument prefix). I could rename it to `example_channel_name` if you prefer.

> But the more important point is, you need the instrument name because you're embedding FQCNs (Fully-qualified channel name like `dev1/4`) in the returned struct/namedtuple, in the case of single-instrument sessions.

Not really, I'm embedding FQCNs in the case of **independent-channels** sessions, which may be single-instrument or multi-instrument. This is also why I need `first_channel_name` to determine whether the session is an independent-channels session as mentioned earlier.

> I think you decided to embed FQCNs in single-instrument sessions because you need to take care of mixing i.e. an input like 4,Dev1/1:2'.

Nope, I'm embedding FQCNs in single-instrument session only when the session is an independent-channels session. In fact, non-FQCNs will be embedded in single-instrument sessions that are non-independent-channels:

```
>>> with nidcpower.Session(resource_name="dev1", channels="", options="Simulate=1, DriverSetup=Model:4162; BoardType:PXIe", independent_channels=False) as smu:
...     with smu.initiate():
...         smu.channels["4,1:2"].measure_multiple()
...
[Measurement(voltage=0.0, current=1e-05, in_compliance=None, channel='4'), Measurement(voltage=0.0, current=1e-05, in_compliance=None, channel='1'), Measurement(voltage=0.0, current=1e-05, in_compliance=None, channel='2')]
```

This **decision** was made based on the convention of the existing `get_channel_names()`, where **FQCNs are returned for independent-channels session** (regardless of whether it is single-instrument or multi-instrument) and **non-FQCNs are returned for non-independent-channels session** (this will always be single-instrument).

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/80089084/177119556-5e70b074-05b5-445b-a02a-b9d013726d59.png)

You could use `read_bytes(-1)` to read all availabe bytes.

I see a problem here:
1. you switch on/off
2. the device responds with a message (buffer contains 7 bytes)
3. you send another command
4. you read (executed immediately afterwards). As there are already 7 bytes in the buffer you read those (wrong ones): ERROR
5. Now the device response arrives with the correct answer, but nobody reads it.

For that reason, it is better to put a `read_bytes(-1)` as `check_errors` method and set the `check_set_errors` parameter to True in the switch_on/off commands. Similar to this code (taken from hcp.TC038D, the read method does some error checking as well):
~~~python
    def check_errors(self):
        """To be called from the property setters to read the acknowledgment."""
        self.read()

    setpoint = Instrument.control(
        "R,0x106", "W,0x106,%i",
        """The setpoint of the oven in °C.""",
        check_set_errors=True,
        get_process=lambda v: v / 10,
        set_process=lambda v: int(round(v * 10)),
    )
~~~

Digging a bit more, I realized we may be already doing the check correctly in the code 😃 

`Service::GetInstanceName()` returns the full service instance name and not just the "instance label". So we are checking for full name (e.g. `abc._serv1._udp` in  `host.HasServiceInstance(service.GetInstanceName()`).

```c++

        /**
         * This method gets the full service instance name of the service.
         *
         * @returns  A pointer service instance name (as a null-terminated C string).
         *
         */
        const char *GetInstanceName(void) const { return mDescription->mInstanceName.AsCString(); }
```

My suggestion is we try to harmonize our use of the terms "name" and "label" in the code (I think we may be using it in most places) 
- Basically "name" always refers to full name (not a single label)
- And use the term "label" when we refer to just one label (e.g. service instance label, or `abc` in `abc._serv1._udp`).

this code actually fixes a long standing bug in py3status, where setting output_format to anything other than i3bar causes the i3status integration to hang indefinitely or crash. 

py3status starts an i3status process internally, and parses its output to support i3status modules. This parsing requires the output to be the i3bar-compatible json format. if i3status produces any other output, which it will when output_format is set to anything other than i3bar, then the integration will break.

As a consequence, to make the output_format setting meaningful for py3status, it is necessary to overwrite the configured value in the generated config file before passing it to i3status.

This does make use of `Lazy`, but it's [these `Lazy`s](https://github.com/allenai/allennlp/blob/eb9abcf370fed33dc0e47b8420c2f5b96de0de70/allennlp/commands/train.py#L532-L535) that get leveraged.  By the time we get to this class, the `Vocabulary` has already been constructed, so we don't need another `Lazy` annotation (for the same reason that `Model.__init__` also doesn't need `Lazy[Vocabulary]` - that gets passed in using the `**extras` stuff in `FromParams`, seen in this code in [this line](https://github.com/allenai/allennlp/blob/eb9abcf370fed33dc0e47b8420c2f5b96de0de70/allennlp/commands/train.py#L629)).  `Lazy` is only necessary if you have multiple arguments to a *single function* that have sequential dependencies, like a `Vocabulary` that has its own parameters but depends on training data for its construction.

`nextUpdate` is the latest time when this OCSP staple will be valid, and is basically promising "an updated OCSP response will be available before this time". 

Apache uses 1 hour TTL internally, so it'll attempt to refresh the staple every hour, triggered by incoming request. We want to prevent this hitting basically ever as Apache does poor job in validating the responses or retrying before replacing the existing response.

For Let's Encrypt the `nextUpdate` is 7 days from `thisUpdate`, and I'm not 100% sure about when the new one will be available, but it has been days in my tests.

I chose half of the time between now and `nextUpdate` as TTL value as it will scale, while it should never be low enough for Apache's internal response fetching to trigger.

I believe there is still another use case that this approach doesn't cover though. What if you have annotations for some clinical events (e.g., sleep stages, seizures, etc.) that are not necessarily of the same length? For example, you might have a sleep stage annotation that is 10-min long, followed by another one that is only 60-s long, etc. Let's say you are interested in extracting as many non-overlapping 30-s windows as possible inside each annotation. Currently, this function would only let you extract windows inside a fixed region following each event found (specifically, between `trial_start_offset_samples` and `trial_stop_offset_samples`), that is the same across all annotations. In this case, you would then be stuck with only extracting windows out of the first 60 s of each sleep stage annotation.

A possible solution would be to use the `chunk_duration` argument of `events_from_annotations` which already covers this scenario.  The function `_compute_supercrop_inds` could then be repurposed to find the `i_trials`, `i_supercrop_in_trials`, `starts` and `stops` for each event spit out by `events_from_annotations`. Another possibility would be to reimplement the `chunk_duration` functionality into `_compute_supercrop_inds` if that's simpler for now. Eventually though we could see whether there would be interest on MNE's side to add these kinds of outputs to `events_from_annotations`.

By symmetry, I mean the fact that cases 1,2,4,8 are just rotations of each other, and furthermore these are the same as 14,13,11,7 with + and - swapped (you might think of this as reflection in the Z coordinate). Whatever we decide is the correct treatment for one of these cases can be transferred to the others by applying the correct symmetry.

My thinking was that the outputs in case 1 (top-left segment) and case 9 (top-left and bottom-right segments) both include the top-left line segment, so we could unambiguously output this segment; but I see now that I might have been too hasty: 9 is one of the ambiguous "saddle points", so it could instead output top-right and bottom-left.

I think there are probably still some cases where this really isn't ambiguous if we take `vertex_connect_high` in to account; but I also might just be trying to get too clever here - since contours are going to be cut off by the mask edge anyway, losing a single pixel probably isn't a big deal.

> why this and not: (k1 && k2.isKnown) || runWithUnknowns?

These two expressions are not equivalent. The expression in the code reads "the result is known if the inner `isKnown` resolves to `true` and either the outer `isKnown` resolves to `true` or if we are explicitly processing unknown values. Your suggestion reads "the result is known if the inner `isKnown` and the outer `isKnown` both resolve to `true` or if we are explicitly processing unknown values. The former only allows the result to be known if the inner result is known; the latter allows the result to be known even if the inner result is unknown.

For the `finalize()` method here, it seems valid to perform this check without explicit inter-process locking, but I'm concerned that propagating the result of `.is_finalized` elsewhere may make things harder to reason about (unless we're absolutely sure that the resulting directory will never be moved or deleted). In particular, for this segment later on:
```python
      if not build_result.is_built:
        TRACER.log('Building {} to {}'.format(build_request.source_path, build_result.dist_dir))
        unsatisfied_build_requests.append(build_request)
      else:
        TRACER.log('Using cached build of {} at {}'
                   .format(build_request.source_path, build_result.dist_dir))
        install_requests.extend(build_result.finalize_build())
```
I think this code seems mostly legitimate, but what's a little confusing to me is how `AtomicDirectory#finalize()` will no-op if `.is_finalized` is true, but then in this excerpt, `BuildResult` will immediately call `.finalize_build()`, which calls `self.atomic_dir.finalize()` (which would then be a no-op).

One possible way to refactor this might be to convert the `.finalized` check into an `@contextmanager`, or perhaps to provide some hook method when constructing an `AtomicDirectory` which is run when the finalization occurs.

I'm not super opinionated about this, and I think memorializing the kind of guarantee we want in the `finalized()` method and/or the `.is_finalized` property like @Eric-Arellano suggested would be sufficient here.

The implementation of the function is printed below. It contains quite a bit of custom logic.

```python
def _capture_output(args, capfd, expect_output=False):
    """
    Calls chartpress given provided arguments and captures the output during the
    call.
    """
    # clear cache of in memory cached functions
    # this allows us to better mimic the chartpress CLI behavior
    chartpress.image_needs_building.cache_clear()
    chartpress.image_needs_pushing.cache_clear()
    chartpress.latest_tag_or_mod_commit.cache_clear()

    # first flush past captured output, then run chartpress, and finally read
    # and save all output that came of it
    _, _ = capfd.readouterr()
    chartpress.main(args)
    out, err = capfd.readouterr()
    if not expect_output:
        assert out == ""

    # since the output was captured, print it back out again for debugging
    # purposes if a test fails for example
    header = f'--- chartpress {" ".join(args)} ---'
    footer = "-" * len(header)
    print()
    print(header)
    print("out:")
    print(out)
    print("err:")
    print(err, file=sys.stderr)
    print(footer)
    return err
```

This is a very creative approach using the bash completer, however, since these env vars are for the process and gets inherited by `az` and then removed, it would be better to use .NET API to start the process and leveraging https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/api/system.diagnostics.processstartinfo.environmentvariables?view=net-6.0#system-diagnostics-processstartinfo-environmentvariables

It would look something like:

```powershell
$env = @{
  _ARGCOMPLETE = 1
  COMP_TYPE = 9
  _ARGCOMPLETE_IFS = " "
}

$psi = [System.Diagnostics.ProcessStartInfo]::new($AzCommand)
$psi.RedirectStandardError = $true
$psi.RedirectStandardOutput = $true

foreach ($var in $env.keys) {
  $psi.Environment.Add($var, $env.$var)
}

$az = [System.Diagnostics.Process]::Start($psi)
$az.Start()
$az.WaitForExit()
$completionResult = $az.StandardOutput.ReadToEnd()
```

```suggestion
Since these are fully type safe they are often easier to implement than `mode='before'` model_validators.
```
It's easy for us to read the docs and understand when we are using the words "before", "after", "validator" as referencing specific concepts, but I think it might be a bit harder for people needing to go through these docs, so I would suggest trying to make it more clear when it's a reference to a specific concept (e.g., `mode='before'`, or `@model_validator`) rather than using "normal" language, since I think it will cost new readers some cognitive overhead to parse 
> After validators are instance methods ...
as
> `mode='after'` validators are instance methods ...
rather than
> After the point in time that validators are instance methods ...

Obviously they won't stay confused for long, I just think it's a pattern we can try to follow to make this all that much easier to read.

My concern is: will there be cases where we want to make assertions about a contract's Storage before it has been written to?  By "make assertions," I mean something like this:
https://github.com/trailofbits/manticore/blob/55e2486f4791757b77fd16b0dfe65d4ecb6e3e80/tests/other/test_smtlibv2.py#L146-L148
Or, more concretely, would we want to allow something like this?
```
    cs.add(world_state.get_storage_data(address, offset) == ord('A'))
```
Right now, this won't work if `address`'s Storage has not been written to.

I *think* we want this to not work. We currently take a "closed world" approach in the sense that the only addresses we acknowledge in the overlay are those to which we have observed writes. While this may sound restrictive, it actually facilitates solving certain types of constraint sets. An example is here:
https://github.com/trailofbits/manticore/blob/eecd9b5a67b25e2cabe55ffc783e7a0ea65d7bde/tests/ethereum/test_storage.py#L235-L238
That `assertFalse` would fail if we did not have this "closed world" assumption.

Having said that, there is a tiny voice in my head that says this may not be the right approach, i.e., there may be situations where we want to allow assertions on a contract's Storage, even though we have not observed writes to it.

Adding to this, `set_storage_data` requires a `constraints` parameter. So requiring it also for `get_storage_data` does not seem that jarring.

For context, the reason we return a new context here is just to maintain the API - otherwise I'd prefer to return `None`. That said, you're right that returning a new context every time is problematic for something like the following

```python
ctx = tracer.get_call_context()
ctx.sampling_priority = USER_KEEP
...
with tracer.trace(...): ...

with tracer.trace(...): ...
```

Before this PR: the first trace will get the sampling priority applied but the second won't because the context is cleared after a trace finishes. (Even this seems unintuitive because the context is outside the scope of the first trace)

With this PR: neither of the traces will get the sampling priority applied which also seems wrong. This is why I'd prefer to return `None` from `get_call_context` so it's clear that there isn't a context.

So what this makes me think is that a breaking change should be make so that `get_call_context` can return `None`.

Create a single `InternalSerializedAutoMaterializeRule` class, which contains the following attributes:

- original class name
- string description
- auto materialize decision type (skip, materialize, discard)

Then, when constructing an AutoMaterializeRuleEvaluation, we pass in this serialized rule object, rather than the actual AutoMaterializeRule. In the GQL layer, this is enough information to construct our GrapheneAutoMaterializeRule.

The main risk here is that we think of other fields we want to toss on that object in the future (e.g. if we forgot to put the decision type on it, it'd be hard to add it in, whereas the current solution it's just a property of the actual rule)

This is assuming that all kernels are local to the server - which is not the case for Enterprise Gateway - which derives its kernel management from these classes.  In this case, EG will break since the launching process is no longer running.

I would recommend moving the code that gets the memory usage to a method on the individual kernel manager class - this way child classes can do their own means of capturing memory usage.  Unfortunately, Notebook doesn't define a kernel manager class.  If one was added, it would derive from jupyer_client's [`IOLoopKernelManager`](https://github.com/jupyter/jupyter_client/blob/master/jupyter_client/ioloop/manager.py#L26) - then we'd need to fix up the hierarchy usage in Jupyter Kernel and Enterprise Gateway.  I'd be happy to help with this if others agree.  The other option is to abstract the `kernel` class that represents the process.  We do this in Enterprise Gateway with our `process proxy` classes, but that's a bit more work.  We'd then have a 'hook' to place capture code for our remote kernels.

Any way we can make the capture of per-kernel info more abstract would be great.

In the meantime, we should probably make this feature optional and, preferably, off by default so as to not break applications.

The documentation is not clear on this, but normally the connection timeout is per connection (not the total time) this should be apparent when you have slow DNS lookups or DNS problems, in this case the DNS latency is added to the connection timeout as well.

If you use retry=4, each connection has the same timeout. For the successful case there's no difference in behavior, for the case where the connection was established (but faulty) there's also no difference in behavior. But for the case where the server does not respond, and retries are attempted (not sure if it retries in this case, I would assume not) the connection timeout could be 4x as long. But it's easy to test this behavior in fact.

这段代码补丁主要包括以下几个方面的改动和函数添加：

1. 引入模块：从functools导入wraps，从six中导入string_types、moves.queue以及moves.urllib.parse中的parse_qsl和urlparse。

2. split_cmd函数：没有变化。

3. make_file_executable函数：没有变化。

4. 新增函数：parse_device_uri用于解析设备URI获取平台、主机、UUID以及其他参数。

在parse_device_uri函数中，首先使用urlparse函数解析uri，并将结果保存在变量d中。然后提取scheme（平台）、netloc（主机）、path（UUID）等信息，利用parse_qsl函数解析query部分得到参数字典params。如果主机不为空，则将其拆分成列表形式的host。最后返回平台、UUID和params。

对于代码补丁的评审和改进建议，需要结合具体应用场景和代码使用情况进行综合考虑，无法一概而论。但从代码本身看，补丁中引入了新功能函数并对现有代码进行了扩展，可能会涉及到新的bug风险。需要仔细测试和验证新功能函数的正确性和稳定性，并确保与现有代码的兼容性。同时，也可以考虑添加更详细的注释和文档说明，以便其他开发人员理解和使用这些新增函数。

Hm.  Interesting.  I can certainly imagine this style of test.  It seems like it's closer to the ad hoc end of the testing spectrum.  Test methods call this method as they please to trigger certain codepaths in the client that the test wants to verify.

The upside is plenty of flexibility and minimal per-message/test coding overhead.  The downside is that it's unverified server behavior and the more tests you have, the more you repeat yourself.

The obvious alternative at the other end of the spectrum is to use your real server protocol class.  Then your real server protocol has to play nicely ... and you don't duplicate messaging logic in the tests because you're relying on the real implementation.

we can start with this advice from [here](https://herbsutter.com/2020/02/17/move-simply/):

>To pass a named object `a` as an argument to a `&&` “move” parameter (rvalue reference parameter), write `std::move(a)`. That’s pretty much the only time you should write std::move, because C++ already uses move automatically when copying from an object it knows will never be used again, such as a temporary object or a local variable being returned or thrown from a function.

a named object is an lvalue, so we would need to cast it to an rvalue reference which is just what std::move does.

in this code, we also deal with functions which take a string (or some other type we might not want to copy) by value, for example, `OpSchema::Attribute::Attribute()`. this way, we can avoid writing two versions of this constructor, one taking a `const string&` (where we copy the parameter) and the other taking a `string&&` (where we move from the parameter). so we can use `std::move(variable_name)` here, in the spirit of the advice above, to move into the by-value parameter instead of copy into it.

some more details on this pass-by-value pattern [here](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/51705967/advantages-of-pass-by-value-and-stdmove-over-pass-by-reference).

as another example, the compiler will move without us writing std::move if we pass an rvalue as the parameter:
`Attribute attr(GetName(), ...);`

> It's interesting to note that `powermetrics` output shows it's able to return per-CPU frequencies. Current psutil's [cpu_freq() implementation](https://github.com/giampaolo/psutil/blob/7eadee31db2f038763a3a6f978db1ea76bbc4674/psutil/arch/osx/cpu.c#L113-L140) on OSX doesn't do that (same on Windows and OpenBSD).

Yeah, I've been chasing the "current frequency" nut for years.  I've always thought that CPU frequency was something like Heisenberg's uncertainty principle.  You change it by measuring it. :)

`powermetrics` is useful but is also closed-source so I don't know how it works, but it isn't really suitable for command-line parsing:
 - it polls every 5 seconds 
 - it requires sudo
 - it has differing output per-core.

That said, I will show you how powermetrics gets its "current frequency" and try to amplify your comment on your review,
> I am not sure I understand what is going on here. It seems it's based too much on guessing.

I'd prefer to use the term "reverse engineering". :) There are plenty of ARM CPU IORegistry dumps out there to validate these particular calls from iPhones, and I've personally tested them on both M1 and M2.

The pairs of numbers are most definitely frequency values.  I just wrote a quick program to print out the pairs on my M2:
```
voltage-states5-sram
702000000 / 790
948000000 / 790
1188000000 / 790
1452000000 / 806
1704000000 / 834
1968000000 / 859
2208000000 / 896
2400000000 / 915
2568000000 / 931
2724000000 / 978
2868000000 / 1009
3000000000 / 1062
3132000000 / 1087
3264000000 / 1140
3360000000 / 1140
3408000000 / 1140
3504000000 / 1140
3528000000 / 1196
3696000000 / 1196

voltage-states1-sram
912000000 / 590
1284000000 / 625
1752000000 / 678
2004000000 / 706
2256000000 / 778
2424000000 / 831
```
As I mentioned before I'm not sure what the second number is, but the first one is definitely frequency, as the more detailed `powermetrics` output shows. It gives percentage of time (probably over that 5-second polling interval) that the CPU was operating at each of its possible speeds.

CPU 0 (an E-core) spent 100% of its time in the lowest 912 MHz setting; but you can see the other values exactly line up with the voltage states IOReg value.
```
CPU 0 frequency: 912 MHz
CPU 0 idle residency:   0.00%
CPU 0 active residency: 100.00% (912 MHz: 100% 1284 MHz: .00% 1752 MHz:   0% 2004 MHz:   0% 2256 MHz:   0% 2424 MHz:   0%)
```
CPU 4 (a P-core) is a bit more insteresting, the 751 MHz "current frequency" is a weighted average of the possible frequencies by the amount of time spent in them:
```
CPU 4 frequency: 751 MHz
CPU 4 idle residency:  15.76%
CPU 4 active residency:  84.24% (702 MHz:  68% 948 MHz:  16% 1188 MHz:   0% 1452 MHz: .03% 1704 MHz: .00% 1968 MHz: .13% 2208 MHz: .03% 2400 MHz: .01% 2568 MHz:   0% 2724 MHz:   0% 2868 MHz:   0% 3000 MHz:   0% 3132 MHz:   0% 3264 MHz:   0% 3360 MHz: .00% 3408 MHz:   0% 3504 MHz:   0% 3528 MHz:   0% 3696 MHz:   0%)
```
Note that 751 MHz is only an average for the 84.24% of time that it was active.  What was its frequency when idle?  Zero?  So perhaps the average should really be 629 MHz?   

TLDR: "Current Frequency" on any CPU is too poorly defined to really measure.  However, these outputs show these voltage states as a reliable source for the possible settings.

Is it documented these outputs are always in ascending order?  No, I'm guessing. But it would make no sense for outputs like the above to have to sort each time, so it's a reasonable guess that every other 4 bytes is a possible frequency, so index 0 is the min frequency and index (length-8) is the max frequency for that core.

nit: At one point there was a subtle bug in the compiler where an if-elif tree like this one, where each branch was meant to return, actually had a code path that didn't return. With the `raise AssertionError` in the `else` branch, that meant that no error was raised (since the `else` wasn't taken, one of other the branches was taken but didn't return), and instead the function simply returned `None` to the caller ... which promptly exploded elsewhere.

So if all branches are meant to `return`, I'd pull the `AssertionError` out of the else, and make it say `Unreachable state reached` (+ as much contextual info as possible) instead of necessarily blaming the cardinality.

IMO, in Python the general preference is to group classes/functions with related (coherent!) functionality in a single module. For example, scikit-learn also groups multiple estimators in a single module, as long as they belong to the same 'family' of estimators.

In this case, I would argue that these Sensor classes should be included in a single module because they are strongly related to one another. From a user perspective it's also easier to use if you don't have to have a separate import for every single (related) sensor/operator/hook. 

The one-class-per-file preference is more java-esque and seems to be mainly preferred by (ex) Java programmers.

So, the dependencies in the `pyproject.toml` file are not pinned dependencies, they just define version ranges for the dependencies. The pinned dependencies, however, are defined in the `poetry.lock` file instead. And it seems like this PR missed updating the lock file (with command `$ poetry lock`). So, for example, you might end up seeing, in the lock file, something like version `2.3.4`, but `^2.1.3` in `pyproject.toml` (because it only defines a loose dependency, not a pinned one).

We should try to eliminate this since it is effectively a copy.  I think we could use a "double buffer" idea to implement this, that is:

  1. allocate two buffers for all reads (say 4k each), buffer A and buffer B
  2. buffer A is initially assigned as the "read buffer", buffer B is the "leftover" buffer
  3. "read buffer" is passed to network/file layer that supports `readinto`
  4. read buffer is returned filled with data
  5. SBP processing is done, some amount of data is potentially left over, this becomes the "left over buffer", so "read buffer" and "leftover buffer" swap
  6. It's an error if 4k of "left-over" is accumulated and the left-over buffer is flushed

The default implementation of the `transform_batch` is to apply `transform` to each batch passed to `transform_batch` and `yield from`, resulting in a flattened stream (which might still be batched, because some transforms map from `Instances -> Batched`). So transform_batch in this case is actually implementing `StopAfter` at the batch level, which is what would be expected. 

I agree it's a bit odd that this is the default implementation (rather than assuming that `transform_batch` would apply `transform` and just `yield`) - see [this comment](https://github.com/allenai/allennlp/pull/3386/files#diff-41d08074b6dcde4374683117cb26a2cbR46). Fair enough, it's good to check that it's not causing performance regressions. I just had a look. Long story short: on the write, I didn't notice any change, but on the read, it's about 2% slower. IHMO, it's acceptable for the sake of ensuring that all the images are properly handled, while having simpler code.

To be honest, looking at the libtiff code (https://gitlab.com/libtiff/libtiff/blob/master/libtiff/tif_read.c#L512), I was pretty convinced that it could even be a tiny bit faster, because there is an explicit "shortcut" in the C code which does approximately the same checks as in Python (ie, compression is None + no byte swap). Such C code should be faster  than the equivalent Python code, so that should lead to faster code. I was wrong.

Firstly, I tried on some example TIFF images found in the libtiff test cases. However, all the image are tiny (the biggest I found is 140Kb). On such images, the difference is always negligible. So, I tried on a 5Mpx uint16 image (11Mb), and the results (on my laptop, with Python 2.7) with the original code are:
 * read: 11.9 +- 0.2 ms
 * write: 3.9 +- 0.2 ms
With the new code:
 * read: 12.1 +- 0.2ms
 * write: 3.9 +- 0.2 ms

So that's around 2% performance decrease when reading. I've dug a little further, and interestingly, half of the extra time  is due to the extra `self.` in the loop. So changing from `self.ReadEncodedStrip()` to `ReadStrip = self.ReadEncodedStrip... ReadStrip()`, saves about 0.1ms. That's because the image has 2560 rows (and there is one strip per row). This loop is  probably also what explains why the C code is slower: because the checks are done inside the loop instead of outside the loop, so they are done 2560 times, instead of once in Python.
Surprisingly (to me), when writing, there is just a single long strip. So there is no loop, and that's what explains there is no time difference.

So, we could consider putting back the "trick" to avoid the `self.` dereference inside the loop to reduce even further the performance penalty.

For info, here is my (extremely sophisticated :-P) test code:
```
from libtiff import TIFF
imfn = "test-big-16b-gs.tiff"
tif = TIFF.open(imfn, mode='r')
%timeit -n1000 image = tif.read_image(); tif.SetDirectory(0)
```

> This ends up silently losing rsc parallelism for all the `zinc-only` scala targets (which is going to be the vast majority of targets), which is why I was thinking splitting the enum up into `-java` and `-scala` versions was a good idea. 

Since this review doesn't include mixed compile support, zinc only compiles of targets with just scala that are not tests isn't supported, so I'm not sure this applies.

> `zinc-only` targets still only depend on the rsc compiles of their dependencies -- except for targets with any java sources, since javac can't yet depend on rsc output.

I think you're saying that the `zinc-only` workflow should use rsc output of their dependencies, apart from java source having targets. I didn't do that here, because I was trying to keep this change to just removing metacp as much as possible. I'd like to have adding additional workflows be separate patches so that their effects on behavior are clearer.

The current workflows are
- rsc then zinc: eg the current rsc-compatible scala flow. Where rsc jobs depend on rsc and zinc jobs depend on the rsc of the current jobs dependencies
- zinc only: each target is compiled by zinc and requires zinc artifacts on the classpath. Currently this applies to java containing targets and tests.

When we add mixed compile support, we could add another workflow
- zinc with rsc dependencies: for scala targets that fail to compile with rsc, but can build against rsc generated jars.


> I was under the impression zinc invoked javac
Yes. It does. Zinc is just a wrapper for Javac and Scalac. It adds some behavior on top, in particular doing analysis and supporting incremental builds using that.

```
@with_seed()
def test_dropout_with_seed():
    info = np.iinfo(np.int32)
    seed = np.random.randint(info.min, info.max)
    _test_dropout(mx.cpu(), seed)
    _test_dropout(mx.gpu(), seed)
    _test_dropout(mx.cpu())
    _test_dropout(mx.gpu())

def _test_dropout(ctx, seed=None):
    data = mx.nd.ones((100, 100), ctx=ctx)
    dropout = mx.gluon.nn.Dropout(0.5)

    if seed:
        mx.random.seed(seed)
    with mx.autograd.record():
        result1 = dropout(data)

    if seed:
        mx.random.seed(seed)
    with mx.autograd.record():
        result2 = dropout(data)
    if seed:
        # dropout on gpu should return same result with fixed seed
        assert_almost_equal(result1.asnumpy(), result2.asnumpy())
    else:
        # seed not fixed, result should be different
        with assert_raises(AssertionError):
            assert_almost_equal(result1.asnumpy(), result2.asnumpy())
```

>  I think the less special-casing the better, and since this is clearly easy to implement as a rule, maybe that's the way to go?

It does so by using the mutable private fields ("tracker") of a private type. The fact that `_Options` is exposed at all as an intermediate type between `ScopedOptions` and the `OptionsBootstrapper` is an implementation detail that would be good to remove.

> E.g., where would the options currently on ExplainOptionsOptions live?

They could live on a subsystem.

> Concretely, what is the downside to allowing rules to request _Options? It might be fine to have non-public types that are only for internal use.

There are not enough APIs exposed to `@rules` to allow for implementing the graph awareness that is necessary to implement "help for all reachable types" as described in #8884 (and which is needed for the docsite generation). And unless we have a desperate need for allowing rules to introspect the rule graph like that, we should not add APIs to do it. `UnionMembership` has proven to be necessary, but it is intentionally quite limited.

Rather than designing an API to wrap an API on the scheduler for use in exactly one place in a rule, I think that we should consume that API outside the engine.

If the routine was to prematurely exit any time after line 27 (`defer lock.Unlock()`), but _before_ the mutex was locked on line 52, we would have a runtime exception due to trying to unlock an unlocked mutex.

There are only two real possible solutions, and they both would be at line 51 where the mutex is locked:

First option:

```
lock.Lock()
defer lock.Unlock()
```

or second option:

```
lock.Lock()
lock.Unlock()
```

I prefer the first of these because it is simpler, and there is no reason for the lock to be held while the test failure condition is checked and reported.

>The fraction of transmitted light to incident light is called the incident angle modifier (IAM).

Is this what Marion says? Because strictly speaking, I don't think this is true. At normal incidence, there might be 4% internal reflection, meaning transmitted light is only 96%. You can derive this directly from [Fresnel's equation at normal incidence](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fresnel_equations#Normal_incidence):

```
R = ((n1 - n2)/(n1 + n2))**2
```

So if you look at the [Sandia Array Performance model](https://prod-ng.sandia.gov/techlib-noauth/access-control.cgi/2004/043535.pdf) the IAM or f2 is defined as the following ratio:

```
f2_aoi = Isc(aoi=aoi) / Isc(aoi=0)
```

That sequence diagram could do with some tuning.

Basically, on the consumer side of things the underlying Pact binaries aren't really involved in the testing - just serialisation of the contract (and reification of the message)

1. Consumer sets its expectations for what it can receive from a message provider and provides a way to invoke the message consumer (e.g. registering the handler function)
2. The framework is responsible for taking the input, stripping away the matchers etc. (reification) and then invoking the message consumer function with the expected input message
3. The framework checks if the message handler succeeds or fails. If it fails, the test ends. If it passes, it calls the binary to append the interaction to the contract

So basically, if the message handler doesn't fail, we consider the test successful. It's not quite the same as the HTTP pact, where the framework performs assertions on the HTTP request the consumer performs etc.

Sans cette variable `calcul_lissage_cehr`, le lissage pourrait être automatiquement appliqué alors qu'il n'y pas lieu (typiquement lorsque les données ne sont pas fournies pour les années antérieures à l'année d'imposition), pouvant conduire à retourner un montant de CEHR inattendu (et faux), sans qu'il soit possible d'obtenir le montant réel attendu (sans lissage).

Or, le recours à cette variable assure que par défaut, le lissage ne soit pas appliqué, tout en permettant à chaque utilisateur, en connaissance de cause, d'activer expressément l'application du lissage.

Je vois en fait deux alternatives :
- soit on conserve la partie du code permettant une application du lissage, et le recours à la variable `calcul_lissage_cehr` me semble indispensable pour éviter un calcul de CEHR faux lorsqu'aucune donnée n'est fournie sur N-1 et/ou N-2 (sauf encore une fois à être en mesure de différencier un RFR de 0 € d'un RFR non renseigné/calculé) ;
- soit on prend le parti de ne pas calculer du tout de lissage dans Openfisca-France.

Enfin, sur la différence entre vecteur et scalaire, je comprends qu'un vecteur permet de renseigner cette variable individuellement pour chaque contribuable d'une même simulation, tandis qu'un scalaire serait défini une seule fois pour l'ensemble de la simulation (désolé si je dis une bêtise, je découvre ces sujets...).

I think the caller needs to provide another name only when there is a name conflict (i.e. response code is `kResponseNameExists`).

Another issue with name conflicts is that there is no way for the SRP server to specify which name has been already taken. So the client cannot correctly notify the caller whether a new host or service instance name is needed. I think one solution is to have the SRP server includes the conflicting `Instruction` (or just a single "Delete All" RR which includes the conflicting name) in the response, so that the client knows which name it needs to update.

Interesting idea... Didn't know we can choose the max split from `split` API.

```python3
def _parse_filter(filter: str) -> Tuple[str, PredicateType, SupportedFilterType]:
    """Parse the filter string to a tuple of key, preciate, and value."""
    # The function assumes there's going to be no key that includes "="" or "!=".
    # Since key is controlled by us, it should be trivial to keep the invariant.

    predicate = None
    min_index = len(filter)
    predicate_candidates = {
        "=": filter.split("=", 1),
        "!=": filter.split("!=", 1)
    }

    for pred, kv in predicate_candidates.items():
        # If it is splittable by a given filter delimeter.
        if len(kv) == 2:
            k, _ = kv

            # Choose the lowest length key because
            # that's the first found delimeter from the filter.
            # E.g., key=abc!=value -> first found delimeter is `=`
            # which has lower index.
            if len(k) < min_index:
                predicate = pred
                min_index = len(k)

    if not predicate:
        raise ValueError(
            f"The format of a given filter {filter} is invalid: "
            "Cannot find the predicate. "
            "Please provide key=val or key!=val format string."
        )

    k, v = predicate_candidates[predicate]
    if len(k) == 0 or len(v) == 0:
        raise ValueError(
            f"The format of a given filter {filter} is invalid: "
            f"Cannot identify key {k} or value, {v} (it is empty). "
            "Please provide key=val or key!=val format string."
        )

    return (k, predicate, v)
```

You would need to define a separate function for it. Changing the return value alone is not sufficient for the compiler to differentiate two function signatures.

You could reasonably write an internal function (say `populateNeighborList`) that contains almost all the logic of `toNeighborList` that accepts a pointer to a `NeighborList` and simply writes the data into that `NeighborList`. The existing `toNeighborList` function would make a `new NeighborList()` and pass that directly through to `populateNeighborList`. You could then write a new function that creates a `nl = NeighborList()` on the stack, then calls `populateNeighborList(*nl)` to update this object directly, then return it by value. However, in this case the new function would still be returning by value, so you'd have to look at the precise rules for copy elision in C++ to verify that you can depend on compilers to optimize out the copy operation; otherwise you'd be back where you started, except that the copy operation would be implicit on the function return rather than explicit inside `makeDefaultNlist`.

So maybe a recap of what happened will help us.

1. I originally discovered an issue (#1540) where jedi was providing incorrect completions when working with different host and client environments. In particular, inherited environment variables such as `PYTHONHOME`, or `*PYENV*` could cause incorrect `sys.path` for the client. Naturally, I thought that a clean environment would be best. #1546  then changed Jedi's behavior from passing the host `os.environ` to the client (`Popen`'s default behavior) to forcing a clean environment.
1. Except that turned out to cause other issues (#1617) where some circumstances _required_ a specific environment variable (`PYTHONHOME`), _but not necessarily_ inherited from the host `os.environ`. Naturally, I thought that we just need to allow the user to explicitly pass environment variables. #1619 then changed Jedi's behavior from forcing a clean environment to setting a clean environment by default with an option for the user to pass explicit environment variables.
1. Then #1629 revealed my mistake. I should have simply provided the option to explicitly pass environment variables to `Popen`. This would have preserved `Popen`'s (and Jedi's) original behavior as default (client inherits host `os.environ`) and resolved #1540, in which case #1690 and #1629 would not have arisen. In my defense, however, this solution was not possible for me originally since I am working with Jedi through [python-language-server](https://github.com/palantir/python-language-server) which does not provide the option to pass through environment variables to Jedi `Environment`s. This will be remedied by palantir/python-language-server#822.

So, bottom line (IMHO)...just reflect `Popen` behavior: client environments will inherit `os.environ` by default or optionally take user provided environment variables.
- Restores Jedi's behavior pre 0.17.1 and fixes #1629 (and precludes any other similar issues that my exploits may have caused :wink:)
- Resolves issues such as #1540 and #1617 by allowing the user to pass explicit environment variables to the client if necessary.

Also, I think that you are correct that `self._env_vars = None` should be sufficient. If the default behavior is to pass only specific variables from the host `os.environ`, we may be inviting issues similar to #1629.

@raulchen and I discussed this offline. Because the thread-safety of core worker APIs is not guaranteed, a worker can easily get crashed if accessing core worker APIs in another threading while the core worker is shutting down. This issue applies to both Java and Python. For now, we assume that users won't do this on purpose. In the long term, we should add some kind of protection in core worker APIs.

In this PR, we'll add a lock at Java level to prevent ref counting GC and `Ray.shutdown()` running simultaneously. In this way, we won't need the additional lock in `core_worker.cc`, so impacts on Python workers should be minimal.

The idea of the exception handling in the worker (as mentioned above) is that the worker will detect if there was an error on delivery via the transport and in this case, the events are put back to the database so that delivery will be tried again. By putting the events back to the database, they are also persisted in case the application shuts down between the failed delivery attempt and the next attempt.
While there is no real backoff logic, the `QUEUED_EVENTS_FLUSH_` settings in `constants.py` can be used to tune the sending behavior.
And this works for all transports in the same way.

```
if __name__ == '__main__':

```

New behavior:
- either `lr` is used (`lr_schedule` is None).
- or `lr_schedule` is used (not None), in which case `lr` is ignored.

Old behavior:
example: lr=100 lr_schedule=[[1M, 0.001], [2M, 0.0003]]
-> for the first 1M steps, we use lr=100, then 0.001, linearly decreasing to 0.0003 (at ts=2M)

I find this behavior very confusing b/c it depends then on the timesteps I define in my schedule (if I define ts=0 in my schedule, then `lr` is never used b/c the schedule "kicks" right in, but if I start my schedule definition at some ts >0, then `lr` is used for the first n timesteps).

We could be even more strict and force users to set either one of the setting to None.
Only allowed: `lr=None, lr_schedule is not None` OR `lr is not None, lr_schedule=None`.

It's technically possible to try to treat them all the same, and try running `init` and `apply`. There are cases where that doesn't work, there's a bit more in the linked issue.

I think making all modules targettable as deployments has consequences for Pants. It means that every module will generate its own resolve and will be targetted by `generate-lockfiles` and `export` (future plans). That might not be very useful if there's one root deployment which has several internal modules. [ref](https://pantsbuild.slack.com/archives/C0D7TNJHL/p1684817389109879) (I think we could make resolves which include multiple modules by implementing logic to solve the requirements and search for the modules, but that's a lot of implementation on our part.)

I personally think it makes sense to have a separate target type for deployments. This lets us have multiple deployments which reference the same Terraform module but with different backend configs and variables. Something like [this layout](https://www.codurance.com/publications/2020/04/28/terraform-with-multiple-environments#Using-Folders-Method-2) would let us have a build file something like
```python
terraform_module()
terraform_deployment(name="DEV", vars_file="dev/terraform.tfvars")
terraform_deployment(name="TEST", vars_file="test/terraform.tfvars")
terraform_deployment(name="PROD", vars_file="prod/terraform.tfvars")
```
It also lets me show my team members what's meant as a deployable root module and what's meant to be a shared module.

I think failing to load the repo when you can't access the airbyte instance is generally the correct / desirable behavior. This will only happen on repository reload (generally users in prod won't be clicking this button themselves, the reload will mostly just be triggered by merges into the main branch), and if you can't access the airbyte instance at that time, it'd be pretty bad to silently drop the airbyte assets. asset jobs defined with stuff like AssetSelection.upstream("my_cool_asset") would suddenly no longer include airbyte-related stuff, among other similar issues.

The specific order of events which is maximally disruptive is:

1) user's airbyte service goes down in a pretty bad way (will take active work to fix, rather than just waiting a minute)
2) a PR is merged (or someone manually clicks the reload definitions button), causing the repository definition to be reloaded. this causes an error, as we can't reload this definition from scratch w/o access to the instance
3) while the airbyte issue is being resolved, we want to run jobs that are not related to airbyte

The remedy for this would be for the user to actively remove the airbyte assets from their repo (i.e. pushing a code change) until the root airbyte issue is solved. This is annoying, but I think preferable to silently doing a similar thing on the user's behalf.

Hi @lagru,

> I am assuming that the main benefit here is that it's a real world use case?

Yes, plus it's a *biomedical* use case! :slightly_smiling_face: 

There's also a significant difference in the approach. In the existing inpainting example, since it's made *ad hoc* (with defects added afterwards), we have a *ground truth* (which we can compute error or accuracy against): We can therefore demonstrate (and quantify) the performance of our inpainting algorithm (based on the biharmonic equation).

In the real world, this is usually not the case: We don't know what's actually under the dust! Although, we could try and inpaint areas which are *not* dust-covered, compare the result with the ground truth and, hence, estimate the accuracy in this setting (for these images)...

In this sense, the two examples are not the same kind of demos. Which prompts the idea that we should update the title of the existing example, so it's less generic than just "inpainting."

In this code it returns an approximate date, for example: "3 days ago", I managed to modify the code to extract the exact date of the video, however it cannot identify if the last upload was a short video.

Here is the modified code to extract the exact upload date of the last video.

```python

@property
def last_updated(self) -> Optional[date]:
    """Extract the date of the last uploaded video.

    The API does not provide the date of the last update of the channel,
    so we extract the date of the most recent video published.

    The page's html only provides an approximate date, like "3 months ago",
    so we extract the exact date from the video player.

    :return: Date of last uploaded video.
    :rtype: datetime.date
    """
    self.html_url = self.videos_url

    try:
        # On channels that do not have any video available,
        # it will generate an exception which leads to the return of None
        last_video_content = self.initial_data['contents']['twoColumnBrowseResultsRenderer']['tabs'][1][
            'tabRenderer']['content']['richGridRenderer']['contents'][0]['richItemRenderer']['content'][
            'videoRenderer']
    except (IndexError, KeyError):
        return None
    try:
        # We recovered the player of the last loaded video
        innertube_response = InnerTube(client='WEB').player(last_video_content['videoId'])

        # We extract the exact date of the last uploaded video.
        # Example: 2023-05-11.
        publish_date = innertube_response['microformat']['playerMicroformatRenderer']['publishDate']
        return date.fromisoformat(publish_date)
    except (IndexError, KeyError):
        # If unable to retrieve exact date, return raw string.
        # Example: 1 day ago.
        return last_video_content['publishedTimeText']['simpleText']
```

We can try to extract the data from the last video and the last published short and compare the date of it, but I don't think it would be very interesting.

TL;DR: I removed this line, and in line 218 of `_client_base_async.py`, I changed `uamqp_transport = kwargs.pop("uamqp_transport", False)` to `uamqp_transport = kwargs.get("uamqp_transport", False)`. This way, the boolean is not passed in explicitly. It's passed in implicitly in `kwargs`. Answering your questions below.

1) We're tracking the boolean (i.e. passing it into `Configuration`) so that we can set two properties in the configuration object: 1) hostname and 2) custom_endpoint_address. I'm not sure if this is what you were thinking, but we're not setting a new `uamqp_transport` property in Configuration (i.e. we use `uamqp_transport` locally in the Configuration constructor, and don't set self.uamqp_transport).
2) In the snippet above, I assumed that `uamqp_transport` was not being passed in inside the ClientBaseAsync constructor to super(). So, just in that snippet, I was checking if the amqp_transport was passed in, b/c that would tell us that it was being called by ClientBaseAsync. And if that was the case, then we would still need to set `uamqp_transport` based on the type of the `amqp_transport` (to later be passed into Configuration). The only reason we were checking sync vs. async is that we'd need to set uamqp_transport if async. If sync, we can call `kwargs.pop("uamqp_transport", False).

I mean the case where the user has skipped installing the operator and just tries to use `KubeCluster`. When I say CRD I mean Custom Resource **Definition** which describes the custom resource. These get installed by the helm chart. Pytest also installs/removes these via a fixture for testing.

What you are referring to that `create_namespaced_custom_object` creates is an instance of that CRD, usually just referred to as a *resource*. Without the CRDs Kubernetes has no idea what a `DaskCluster` or `DaskWorkerGroup` even is, and it looks like it returns a 404 in this case.

So my guess is that it returns a 404 if the CRDs are missing, and a 422 if you try and create something that doesn't meet the CRDs expectations.

When scripts are installed in venvs its expected that their shebangs are re-written to point to the current venv interpreter: https://setuptools.readthedocs.io/en/latest/deprecated/distutils/setupscript.html?highlight=scripts#installing-scripts. In the case of a PEX file, this is the executing with the PEX file itself which is what `execute_content` does. So I think a few changes are in order:
1. Python script detection should work exactly like it does for distutils / setuptools - this will address the shebang parsing non-robustness observation aobve.
2. Python script detection only need present a bool result.

Its probably the best to add this parsing / bool directly to DistributionScript which already encapsulates finding these things:
https://github.com/pantsbuild/pex/blob/37c9ff0fa815ac1f03a73d3f2e33fb20352c8843/pex/finders.py#L18-L43

It looks like [poetry](https://python-poetry.org/docs/pyproject/#scripts) and [flit](https://flit.readthedocs.io/en/latest/pyproject_toml.html#scripts-section) only support console scripts so the setuptools / distutils behavior fully defines scripts afaict.

A few thoughts on this actually:
- We might want to just wrap around from the end if trying to shift left at the first item. The fact that we don't wrap implies some kind of ordering to the items, but there is no guarantee there. Example:
    - `"a": [100, 1, 50, 75]`
